name: worktree-diff-detects-stray-direct-edits
tags: worktree, main, dev, test, git status, stray edit, pipeline bypass, skills, task:328fa314
body:
When a multi-worktree repo (dev/test/main) is supposed to only flow one direction (dev→test→main via /deploy), an uncommitted modification found directly in main is a signal someone (human or a prior session) edited main out of band — don't just discard or commit it blindly.

Why: found via a plain `git status` in ~/workspace/claude-hooks that showed two modified skill.md files main had never had committed. Diffing against dev's committed HEAD showed main's uncommitted version was actually *newer* (already contained content dev didn't have yet) — proof the edit bypassed the pipeline rather than main simply lagging behind.

How to apply: before resolving stray uncommitted changes in a downstream worktree (test/main), diff them against the upstream worktree's HEAD to determine direction — if the downstream version is newer/more complete, port the content upstream (dev) and commit there, then discard from the downstream worktree so the pipeline invariant (only /deploy touches main) is restored, rather than committing directly to the downstream branch or discarding the only copy of new work.
